source_items.title: Encore Integration with RAWR
source_items.summary: would it make sense to use https://encore.dev/ as the infra layer underneath the rawr plugin + service + host system? basically make the entire rawr thing deployable on any numb...
source_items.first_prompt:
would it make sense to use https://encore.dev/  as the infra layer underneath the rawr plugin + service + host system?


basically make the entire rawr thing deployable on any number of hosts (always was the plan), and be agnostic as to what’s underneath. The entire host + plugin system should just be distributable as a client (host client), which can be easily configured on any host server.


so that’s where encore comes in — for provisioning dbs, creating networked servers, etc. the stuff that starts to get messy fast


we won’t do this now obviously, but it does illuminate the first concrete step as creating the host client shape. like naming that, which also means we need to define the host sdk shape (which conveniently collects many parts of the repo into one place), which gives us strong semantic understanding of the whole system architecture


unless using encore somehow conflicts with using NX and oRPC and railway. But I figure they either layer or we can create adapters easily
source_items.last_response:
Yes. For your current trajectory, Railway + Nx automation is the simpler and more correct next move than introducing Encore now.


Why: Nx already gives you the thing RAWR most needs at authoring/build/deploy orchestration time — a project graph, task graph, affected runs, and custom executors/commands you can standardize across packages, plugins, and hosts. Railway already gives you the thing you need at the first real infrastructure layer — monorepo deployment support, service-per-package import, Dockerfile-based deployment, internal private networking, and one-click Postgres provisioning. Railway Docs+4Nx+4Nx+4


That combination maps more cleanly onto RAWR than Encore does right now, because RAWR’s real problem is not “how do we define cloud-native services first?” It is “how do we define the host shape, package semantics, plugin runtime, and deployable units cleanly enough that any substrate can run them?” Nx helps you formalize that repo and execution structure without trying to become the semantic owner of the system, while Railway gives you a practical host target without forcing a deeper programming model. Railway Docs+3Nx+3Nx+3


The sanity check is simple.


Baseline: if you removed Encore from the picture entirely, would you still need:
host SDK,
host client,
deployable host runtime,
package/plugin build graph,
service networking,
database provisioning?


Yes. Every one of those still exists. Railway + Nx directly supports those needs today, at least enough for first implementation. Encore does not remove the need to define those RAWR-level abstractions; it mostly adds another opinionated runtime and infra model on top. Nx+3Railway Docs+3Railway Docs+3


So the better sequence is:


First, define the RAWR host contract.
Second, implement a host runtime package.
Third, make that runtime deployable on Railway with Nx-powered build/deploy targets.
Only later ask whether an Encore adapter buys enough to justify its cognitive weight.


That is easier because Railway already supports JavaScript monorepo auto-import and package-specific build/start commands, and Nx already knows how to run graph-aware targets and custom command pipelines. In practice, that means you can standardize things like build, bundle, dockerize, deploy, migrate, seed, start, and smoke as Nx targets, then point Railway services at those outputs or service roots. Railway Docs+2Nx+2


The part that matters most architecturally is this:


Nx should own build orchestration.
Railway should own hosting primitives.
RAWR should own semantics.
oRPC should own service contract / invocation shape.


That layering is clean. Nothing is pretending to be something it is not. Nx is not infra. Railway is not your architecture. oRPC is not your deployment platform. RAWR remains the system model. This is the right separation. Nx+2Railway Docs+2


Where this will break, if you are sloppy, is letting Railway deployment shape leak upward into the host contract. For example, if your host SDK starts assuming Railway env var names, Railway service discovery names, or Railway-only database assumptions, you have already lost the portability you care about. Railway’s internal DNS and service networking are useful, but they should sit behind your host capability layer, not become the public mental model of the system. Railway Docs+1


So the concrete answer is:


Yes, automate host creation with Railway x Nx first.


And more specifically, define something like this:


@rawr/host-contract
capabilities, service registration, config model, lifecycle hooks


@rawr/host-sdk
what packages/plugins import to register routers, jobs, migrations, resources


@rawr/host-runtime
boot logic for a host instance


@rawr/host-client
remote/local client for talking to a host


@rawr/deploy-railway
templates, Dockerfiles, service conventions, env adapters


Then wire Nx targets around them:


nx build host-runtime
nx bundle host-runtime
nx run host-runtime:dockerize
nx run host-runtime:deploy-railway
nx run-many -t build --projects=tag:type=plugin
nx affected -t test,build,smoke


That gives you a real operational model early, with almost no conceptual waste. The exact commands are yours to design, but Nx’s graph/affected/task model is built for this kind of workspace orchestration. Nx+1


On oRPC specifically: Railway does not conflict with it at all. Railway is just hosting/networking/deployment here. Your oRPC routers can live inside host services or plugin services normally, and Railway private networking gives you internal service-to-service communication if you split things across multiple processes. Railway Docs+1


On databases: Railway gives you a zero-config Postgres path immediately, and also supports custom Dockerized database services and persistent volumes when needed. That is enough to avoid early infra thrash. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


My judgment:


For now, Railway + Nx is the right operational substrate.
Encore is still a possible later adapter, but not the move that clarifies the architecture today.
The first real artifact you need is not “Encore integration.” It is the host SDK / host client / host runtime shape.


Assumptions and uncertainty ledger:


I am assuming your primary near-term target is pragmatic deployability and semantic clarity, not maximizing cloud automation sophistication on day one. That assumption fits what you said.
I am also assuming Railway remains an acceptable primary host target for your early distributed story. Its docs support the needed primitives, but if you later need deeper cross-cloud managed infra orchestration, Encore may become more attractive. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


If you want, the next useful step is to draft the exact @rawr/host-contract package shape and the minimal Nx target matrix that would make the whole system feel real.

source_items.title: Branch · Inngest Plugin API Strategy
source_items.summary: Should we wrap all of our plugin APIs in inngest steps? https://www.inngest.com/docs/learn/how-functions-are-executed Think carefully about how this would work with our oRPC cen...
source_items.first_prompt:
Should we wrap all of our plugin APIs in inngest steps?


https://www.inngest.com/docs/learn/how-functions-are-executed


Think carefully about how this would work with our oRPC centric model. Think inside the boundaries and outside the box as needed to discover the truest answer.


Make sure you read from the git nexus branch in the repo. Also see example-todo as the current golden example of a service package. The idea is that plugins use private service clients to build whatever runtime interface is needed to plug into the host.


https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/tree/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install
source_items.last_response:
Thought for 19m 14s


Answer now


Yes to a workflow shell. No to auto-converting naked Inngest functions straight into public oRPC procedures.


The cleanest version is: make workflow plugins look like service packages in authoring ergonomics, but not in public semantics. Service packages expose direct capabilities. Workflow plugins should expose durable control surfaces: start, status, cancel, signal, timeline, maybe result lookup. Your semantic target already separates shared capability logic in packages/*, runtime adapters in plugins/*, and final composition in rawr.hq.ts, with plugins allowed to import packages but not other plugins. That points toward a reusable workflow bridge in packages/* that workflow plugins consume, not a bunch of one-off router glue inside each plugin. GitHub


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


What you have now on the branch is a good halfway state, but it is visibly split. In rawr.hq.ts, the host creates a private service client with createRouterClient(supportExampleRouter, { context: { deps } }), injects that client into both the oRPC and workflow contexts with enrichSupportExampleContext, registers an API plugin router separately from a workflow trigger router, and then independently builds the Inngest functions bundle plus /api/inngest handler from a host-owned Inngest client. The manifest exports these as sibling surfaces: orpc.router, workflows.triggerRouter, and inngest.{client,functions,handler}. GitHub+1


That part is architecturally sound. oRPC’s context model is explicitly a type-safe DI mechanism with initial context and execution context, and createRouterClient is meant for direct server-side invocation with injected context. Inngest, meanwhile, wants nondeterministic work inside step.run() because steps are the durable execution units. So “workflow plugin calls private service clients inside steps” is exactly the right service/workflow boundary. Also, your semantic docs already push semantic ownership into services and treat shared infra as normal but shared write ownership as dangerous, which means workflow plugins should orchestrate service-owned truth, not become another place that owns it. Inngest+3oRPC+3oRPC+3


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


[


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


](/g/g-p-69b4589ea3088191a7e5ca496094e252/c/69b45d87-9544-8321-a97e-de44516945b4?messageId=be16753f-b67e-4094-821d-f2f102eb06b8&conversation_save=1)


What is missing is a single source of truth tying those three surfaces together. Right now, the branch has the pieces, but not the contract that binds them: the docs say coordination.* is the canonical oRPC namespace for workflow CRUD, validation, queueing, run status, and timeline, while rawr.hq.ts only records a workflow capability pathPrefix, a separate triggerRouter, and a separate function factory. You can also see duplication in context bridging: orpc.enrichContext, workflows.enrichContext, and resolveSupportExampleClient being passed again into function creation. That is a strong signal that the abstraction boundary is not finished yet. GitHub+1


So, should you create an “oRPC plugin” that auto-converts Inngest functions into a router? Not literally. In oRPC’s own terminology, a plugin is an interceptor bundle for handlers or links. That is not the main thing you need here. The main thing you need is a workflow descriptor + router generator package built on top of oRPC. oRPC plugins may still help with headers, correlation IDs, or response shaping, but the real artifact is a workflow bridge, not an interceptor plugin. oRPC+2oRPC+2


And I would not derive that bridge from raw Inngest function objects alone. Inngest functions are execution artifacts, not public API contracts. They can be triggered by events, cron, webhooks, or other functions; Inngest itself positions events as the normal external trigger and step.invoke() as the more RPC-like tool for function-to-function composition inside workflows. Not every function should become externally invokable: some are cron-only, some are webhook-only, some are internal fanout/subfunctions, some are wait/signal continuations. Also, inngest.send() gives you event IDs, not a nice durable domain handle for your API callers. So a blind “one function = one procedure” conversion would leak the wrong abstraction. Inngest+3Inngest+3Inngest+3


The better move is this: define workflow operations declaratively, then generate both the oRPC control router and the Inngest functions from that shared descriptor.


Something like this is the right shape:


const triageWorkflow = defineWorkflowOperation({
  capability: "support-example",
  operation: "triage",
  input: TriageRequestSchema,
  public: {
    start: true,
    status: true,
    cancel: true,
    timeline: true,
  },
  trigger: {
    type: "event",
    name: "support-example/triage.requested",
  },
  idempotencyKey: ({ input }) => input.ticketId,
  createFunctions: ({ inngest, services }) => [
    inngest.createFunction(
      { id: "support-example-triage", triggers: [{ event: "support-example/triage.requested" }] },
      async ({ event, step }) => {
        return await step.run("request-triage", () =>
          services.supportExample.triage.items.request(event.data)
        );
      }
    ),
  ],
});


From that descriptor, your shared shell in packages/* should generate four things: a typed oRPC control router, the Inngest functions bundle, host/CLI/UI metadata, and optional internal references for subworkflow composition. That fits your manifest-first target and keeps the plugin itself as a runtime adapter by surface. GitHub+2Inngest+2


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


The generated oRPC side should not mirror the internal function tree. It should expose durable control verbs. At minimum: start, getStatus, getTimeline, cancel, and signal where relevant. If a workflow produces business results that matter later, those results should usually be materialized into a service-owned read model, then read through a normal service/API route. That keeps semantic truth in the service boundary instead of pretending the workflow engine is the source of truth. Inngest’s job is durable orchestration; the service’s job is business truth. Inngest+1


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


[


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


](/g/g-p-69b4589ea3088191a7e5ca496094e252/c/69b45d87-9544-8321-a97e-de44516945b4?messageId=be16753f-b67e-4094-821d-f2f102eb06b8&conversation_save=1)


I would also keep coordination.* as the generic control plane, because your own docs already describe it that way. Workflow plugins should contribute typed capability descriptors and perhaps thin typed “request” procedures, but the generic machinery for queueing, run status, timeline, cancellation, and validation should stay centralized. In practice that means the plugin-generated start procedure can delegate into a generic coordination layer that persists a RAWR workflow request, sends one Inngest event, and returns a RAWR-owned handle. Then coordination.* can serve status/timeline/cancel consistently across capabilities, while /api/inngest remains the framework-native ingress for function execution. GitHub+2Inngest+2


So my concrete recommendation is:


Do build a reusable workflow shell, but put it in packages/*, not as an ad hoc repo plugin.
Do let workflow plugins author typed workflow descriptors and Inngest functions that call private service clients inside step.run().
Do generate an oRPC control router from those descriptors.
Do not auto-convert arbitrary raw Inngest functions into public procedures.
Do not make the public router mirror the internal function graph.
Do make the public API a control plane over durable work.


If you want the sharpest formulation: service packages define capabilities; workflow plugins define orchestrations over capabilities; the oRPC shell exposes control over orchestrations; Inngest executes them durably.


One caveat: I could directly inspect rawr.hq.ts and docs/SYSTEM.md on the branch, but GitHub wasn’t consistently returning deeper nested files, so this recommendation is grounded in the visible branch composition plus the semantic target docs rather than a full read of every nested package file.

source_items.title: NX + RAWR evolution sequence
source_items.summary: What's the difference between Sentry, OpenTelemetry, Pinot, the logger, and so on? I guess overall my question is, like, is there a difference between telemetry and logging and ...
source_items.first_prompt: What's the difference between Sentry, OpenTelemetry, Pinot, the logger, and so on? I guess overall my question is, like, is there a difference between telemetry and logging and whatever Sentry does?
source_items.last_response:
Failed to edit Service-internal-structure-and-ownership


Service-internal-structure-and-ownership


Service internal structure and ownership


What this document is


This document sits one level below the semantic architecture snapshot.


The snapshot locks the main ontology:


packages/ for pure/shared support matter

services/ for semantic service boundaries

plugins/ for runtime embodiments

apps/ for hosts


This document answers the next question:


Once a service exists, how should its internals be structured so the system stays semantically clear, low-ambiguity, and scalable from small to large services?


The goal is not maximal flexibility. The goal is to remove optionality where it creates drift, while preserving a clean path for earned exceptions.



Core rule


A service should be module-encapsulated by default.


That means the default ownership zone inside a service is the module, not a broad shared internal layer.


The service kernel should stay small and explicit:


declarative service definition

service-wide assembly seam

final service router composition

a narrow shared internal layer only when duplication proves it is needed


This gives the service a strong shape:


services/foo/
  src/
    index.ts
    router.ts

    service/
      base.ts
      impl.ts
      router.ts
      shared/
      modules/
        module-a/
        module-b/


The rule is:


module-local first
service-shared second
package extraction last


That is the main law.



Why module-local first is stronger


At small scale, broad shared internal folders feel convenient.
At large scale, they create constant ambiguity.


A flatter service-internal structure creates repeated questions like:


does this belong to this module or the shared repository area?

is this policy local or service-global?

is this schema module-specific or shared?

is this helper generic enough to move upward?


That is exactly the kind of ambient uncertainty the system is trying to eliminate.


Module-local structure is stronger because it makes locality the default. Most questions get the same answer:


keep it with the module unless there is a proven reason not to.


This scales better because the filesystem mirrors semantic ownership.



Canonical service-internal shape


The recommended shape is:


services/foo/
  src/
    index.ts
    router.ts

    service/
      base.ts
      impl.ts
      router.ts
      shared/
        repository/
        db/
        policies/
        middleware/
        providers/
        types/
      modules/
        tasks/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts
          repository/
          db/
          policies/
        tags/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts
        assignments/
          contract.ts
          setup.ts
          router.ts


Meanings


src/index.ts and src/router.ts are thin package boundary surfaces.

src/service/base.ts is the declarative service definition and service vocabulary.

src/service/impl.ts is the service-wide assembly seam.

src/service/router.ts composes module routers once.

src/service/modules/* is the default ownership area.

src/service/shared/* is the earned exception layer for service-internal sharing.



What belongs in the service kernel


The service kernel should stay narrow.


base.ts


Owns:


the service definition

stable context lanes

service-wide metadata vocabulary

service-wide policy vocabulary

builders/factories tied to the service concept


impl.ts


Owns:


service-wide implementer construction

required service-wide middleware attachment

service-wide runtime assembly that should happen exactly once


router.ts


Owns:


final composition of module routers into the service router


These files define the service as a semantic boundary.
They should not become junk drawers.



Module-local by default


Each module should own as much of its own internal truth as possible.


A module may contain:


contract shape

setup/context narrowing

router/handlers

repository logic

db logic

policies

views/mappers

module-local middleware


Example:


modules/tasks/
  contract.ts
  setup.ts
  router.ts
  repository/
    task-repository.ts
  db/
    schema.ts
    migrations/
    queries.ts
  policies/
    can-create-task.ts
    can-update-task.ts


This is preferred over pulling everything upward early.


Why:


it preserves local semantic ownership

it reduces “where does this go?” ambiguity

it makes the module understandable in isolation

it prevents service-shared folders from becoming internal dumping grounds



What service/shared/ is for


service/shared/ is not the default place to author internals.
It is the place for proven service-internal shared seams.


It should be explicitly scarce.


A good service/shared/ exists for things that are:


reused across multiple modules in the same service

not appropriate as public/package-level support matter

still semantically owned by this one service


Examples:


shared service-internal repository primitives

shared service-internal schema/query helpers

shared service-internal policy logic

shared service-local middleware or providers

shared service-internal types


Bad signs:


random helpers landing there by convenience

vague common/, utils/, or lib/ folders

anything public-facing or cross-service landing there


The path should mean something precise:


service/shared/* = shared within this service only


That precision matters.



db vs repository


These only need to be separate when the separation buys clarity.
They are not automatically different folders at every scale.


db


db is for storage mechanics.


Typical contents:


schema definitions

migrations

table metadata

SQL/query-builder fragments

row mapping

transaction helpers

database-specific construction and persistence mechanics


Question answered:


How is data stored and queried mechanically?


repository


repository is for service-facing data access semantics.


Typical contents:


findById

insertTask

listAssignmentsForTask

markCompleted

aggregate load/save patterns

persistence operations expressed in service language


Question answered:


What data operations does this service/module need in its own terms?


Relationship


handler -> repository -> db


That is the ideal layering when the service is large enough to deserve it.


When to keep them combined


For small modules, splitting them can create fake architecture.


Small module rule:


keep db and repository together under one local area if the distinction is not yet buying much.


Larger module/service rule:


split them once storage mechanics and service-facing data semantics are diverging enough to make the split clarifying.


So the rule is:


small = combine locally
large = separate for clarity



What policies are


Policies are named semantic rule decisions.


They are not just convenience predicates.
They are not just errors.
They are not telemetry.


They answer:


Under what rule conditions is this behavior permitted, rejected, constrained, or modified?


Examples:


read-only mode rules

assignment limits

mutation eligibility

task visibility rules

lifecycle/state transition rules

permission checks tied to service semantics


A useful split is:


errors = how failure is expressed
policies = why a decision was made
observability = how that decision is recorded
repository = how data is accessed
db = how storage works


Who can use policies:


handlers

setup/middleware

repositories, when service-owned rule checks are needed before access

service observability/analytics layers

future composed services that must preserve the same decision logic


Policies become worth their own area when rule logic is repeated, important, or semantically named enough that it should not stay buried inline.



Shared database instance vs shared ownership


Multiple services can use the same database instance or the same host-provided dbPool without sharing semantic ownership.


This is the important distinction:


shared infrastructure != shared semantic ownership


Good default


same host: often yes

same Postgres instance: often yes

same dbPool: yes, often

same tables with multiple direct writers: usually no


Preferred model


Each service owns:


its own tables

its own migrations

its own repositories

its own write invariants


Even when several services use the same physical database.


Warning sign


If two services both want direct write authority over the same business tables/entities, one of two things is usually true:


they are actually one service with multiple modules, or

one service should be canonical owner and the other should go through it or through explicitly governed projections.


The shared-host model is not a reason to weaken semantic ownership.



When two “small services” are actually one service


If two candidate services deeply share:


domain entities

write invariants

policy logic

migration ownership

repository semantics


then they may not actually be separate services.


They may be one service with several modules.


This is important because the service structure is already modular. Separation should not be forced prematurely just because the repo can physically hold multiple services.


The question is not “can these be separate?”
The question is “does separate service ownership produce cleaner semantic truth?”


If not, use one service with multiple modules.



Extraction should not remain ambient judgment


One of the biggest risks is leaving “should this internal thing become shared or extracted?” as an open contextual question.


That creates drift.


The rule should be explicit:


Default


Keep internals nested and module-local unless an extraction trigger is hit.


Extraction triggers


Create an extraction candidate when:


a second module needs the same internal mechanism

repeated duplication appears

the internal subsystem becomes more stable than the service-specific logic around it

ownership/lifecycle/governance starts to diverge

the service boundary is being distorted to preserve nesting


Important rule


Hitting a trigger does not mean auto-extract.
It means open a governed extraction candidate.


Flow:


nested by default
  ↓
trigger hit
  ↓
record extraction candidate
  ↓
assess
  ↓
keep nested or extract deliberately


This turns fuzzy architectural taste into a manageable governance process.



Progression law


The whole internal structure should follow this progression:


module-local first
  ↓
service/shared if duplication is real
  ↓
package extraction only if cross-service support value is real


This progression matters because it preserves semantic truth at the smallest scope long enough for the real reuse seams to reveal themselves.


A package should earn its generality.
A shared internal seam should earn its centrality.
A module should keep ownership by default.



What this unlocks later


This internal structure is not just about tidiness.
It supports the later system in several ways.


1. Better graph law


Once modules, service-shared internals, and packages have distinct meanings, later Nx policy becomes much easier to encode without ambiguity.


2. Better scaffolding


Generators can scaffold the kernel pattern consistently because the progression law is explicit.


3. Better agent behavior


Agents get fewer ambient “should I move this?” questions because defaults and exceptions are clear.


4. Better observability and stewardship


Ownership zones become easier to observe, reason about, and assign stewardship over.


5. Better pressure-testing


Cross-boundary work becomes easier to evaluate because service-internal truth is not constantly leaking outward through premature package extraction.



Final law


If there is one rule to carry forward, it is this:


Services should be module-encapsulated by default.
Service-shared internals are earned exceptions.
Package extraction is a governed move, not a convenience refactor.


That is the internal structure law that best matches the larger goals of the system:


low ambiguity

strong semantic ownership

clear scaling from small to large

future graph enforcement

future scaffoldability

future autonomous stewardship


I tried to make a minimal, non-destructive update to the snapshot doc and keep the current structure intact, but the canvas edit failed because the text anchors I targeted did not match the live document exactly. Nothing was changed.


The updates I was trying to integrate were:


make explicit that the system does not want a first-class controller layer, because host/plugin/service/setup/handler already cover that territory cleanly,

add the rule that shared DB infrastructure is fine but shared semantic ownership is not the default,

and clarify that later Nx value is not just graph visibility, but encoding real semantic transitions once they are proven.


I still think those are worth folding in, lightly, and the right place is:


the main snapshot for the controller and shared-DB-ownership mental model at a high level,

the service-internal structure doc for the more precise internal-structure and promotion/extraction implications.


If you want, I’ll do a cleaner targeted rewrite pass next instead of pattern-based patching.

source_items.title: Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node
source_items.last_response:
Thought for 8s


They fit cleanly once you separate three layers:


host substrate, runtime topology, and semantic architecture.


Your earlier conversations were mostly about the first and third layers, and this latest question was about the middle one.


The sharp mapping is:


A host substrate is the thing that gives you infrastructure primitives and deployment mechanics. In your debate, Encore and Railway + Nx both live here. They are not your service model. They are the environment in which your service model gets materialized. Your own snapshot already frames the system around packages / services / plugins / apps, plus host distinctions, as the semantic target above the substrate layer.


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


[


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


](/g/g-p-69b4589ea3088191a7e5ca496094e252/c/69b45d87-9544-8321-a97e-de44516945b4?messageId=be16753f-b67e-4094-821d-f2f102eb06b8&conversation_save=1)


A host in your architecture is the thing that boots and wires the system. It owns shared infrastructure concerns like database pool, telemetry install, config surface, and runtime process composition, while services retain their own contracts and ownership zones. That was already the clean model in the shared-host discussion: “host shares infrastructure; services own semantics.”


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


So in that earlier debate:


Encore as host substrate means: let Encore provision and connect the infra under your host model.

Railway + Nx as host substrate means: Railway runs the actual deployed workloads, and Nx gives you graph law, scaffolding, packaging, orchestration, and generation around them.


Neither of those should redefine what a service or plugin is. They are underneath that.


Now fit the newer terms into that stack.


A service in your project is a semantic/business ownership boundary. It owns contracts, repositories, migrations, and write invariants. It should not be defined by “does it run in its own process?” That is exactly the trap your prior discussion avoided. Multiple services can share one physical Postgres instance and one host-provided dbPool, but they should not casually co-own the same business tables.


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


A server is just a network listener. In Bun or Node, that is an HTTP or RPC entrypoint that binds a port. This belongs to runtime topology, not semantic architecture. One host may boot one server or several. One service may be exposed by one server, several services may be composed into one server, or one service may have no public server at all.


A process is even lower level. It is just an OS runtime unit. One deployed Railway machine/container can run one process or several, depending on platform shape and how you choose to package it. Multiple processes on one machine does not magically mean multiple services. It just means multiple runtime roles.


A sidecar is a companion process that exists to support a main process operationally. In your world, good examples would be telemetry collectors, proxies, maybe a log shipper. It is not a business service. It is not a plugin. It is not a semantic unit. It belongs to host/runtime plumbing.


A multiple servers setup is just one possible runtime deployment shape. It matters only when you decide some part of the system should listen independently, scale independently, or be isolated on the network boundary.


So the right mental compression is:


semantic layer:
  packages
  services
  plugins
  apps
  host SDK / host contract

runtime layer:
  processes
  servers
  workers
  sidecars

substrate layer:
  Encore
  Railway
  Docker
  Kubernetes
  Nx (mostly build/orchestration/graph, not runtime infra itself)


That is the core correction.


Now, specifically for your Encore vs Railway + Nx conversation:


If you use Encore, it is acting as a stronger substrate opinion. It wants to own more of the infra story: service networking, provisioning patterns, maybe database/service composition. That can be valuable, but the risk is that your host model starts bending around Encore’s worldview. If that happens, Encore stops being “substrate” and starts leaking upward into your semantic architecture.


If you use Railway + Nx, the separation is cleaner. Railway is mostly execution/provisioning, Nx is graph/build/scaffold/orchestration, and your own host client / host SDK shape remains the primary semantic abstraction. That is why it likely fits your current direction better: it lets you define the host shape yourself instead of inheriting too much platform semantics.


That matches the earlier pressure you were describing: define the host client shape first, because that reveals the host SDK shape and semantically gathers the system into one place. That is the important move. Once that is stable, Encore or Railway become replaceable implementation substrates rather than architecture-defining choices.


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


So where each term fits:


Host
Your architectural runtime container. Boots plugins and services, injects infra, exposes stable deps like dbPool, telemetry, config. This is your term, and it should remain yours.


Service
Semantic ownership boundary. Owns business truth and writes. May or may not get its own process or server.


Plugin
Attachable capability unit that the host can load. It is above process/server concerns. A plugin might expose a server, spawn a worker, register routes, or just contribute behavior.


Server
A listening runtime endpoint. Usually created by the host from one or more services/plugins.


Process
A concrete runtime unit. The host might run as one process or compose multiple.


Sidecar
Operational support process next to the host or one of its runtime roles. Not part of your core domain model.


Multiple servers
A deployment/runtime shape, not a semantic category.


Multiple services
A semantic architecture choice, not proof of multiple processes or machines.


The clean rule is:


Do not let server, process, or sidecar decide what a service is.


Your earlier docs already point in that direction. Shared host, shared infra, separate service ownership is normal; what matters is semantic ownership and write authority, not physical deployment shape.


Response: NX + RAWR evolution s…


So in practice, for RAWR:


Start by defining the host contract and host SDK.


Then define what a plugin can contribute:
route/server surface, background worker, scheduler, maybe UI/client surface, telemetry hooks, migrations, etc.


Then decide which of those contributions run:
in the main host process,
in separate worker processes,
or with operational sidecars.


Only after that do you choose how much Encore or Railway should automate underneath.


That means the real architecture stack is:


RAWR semantic model
  -> host contract
  -> service/plugin/app/package boundaries
  -> runtime roles (api server, worker, scheduler, sidecars)
  -> substrate implementation (Railway+Nx or Encore)


Not this:


Encore or Railway
  -> therefore services/processes/servers mean X


That second path is backwards and will distort the system.


My blunt read: in your project, services and plugins are first-class semantic units. Servers and processes are host execution details. Sidecars are operational helpers. Encore and Railway are substrate choices. Nx is the graph/governance/build layer that helps make the semantic model enforceable.


That is the correct fit.

source_items.title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node
source_items.last_response:
Thought for 43s


Yes, that mostly makes sense.


The piece you were missing is the center of gravity.


A steward should not be “whatever NanoClaw needs.”
A steward should be a RAWR-native declaration of persistent agency that NanoClaw consumes.


That matches your product docs. RAWR says identity is stewards: persistent agent identities with domain scope, GitHub accounts, and accumulated decision history, not stateless personas. It also says NanoClaw is the agent dispatch runtime, while RAWR owns the opinions about how the pieces compose.


rawr-product-synthesis


rawr-product-synthesis


So the right model is:


RAWR defines the steward.
NanoClaw runs the steward.


That is the clean split.


The steward authoring model should therefore be declarative and RAWR-first. NanoClaw is just one runtime backend that consumes that declaration. That part of your instinct is right.


Where I would push back is on collapsing everything into “the only plugin is a steward.” That is too much responsibility in one noun. It will become mush fast.


A steward should declare:


identity

domain scope

memory bindings

allowed toolsets / skill packs

channel bindings

runtime preferences


But the steward should reference channels and skill packs, not own their implementation.


Why? Because channels and skill packs are reusable infrastructure or capability surfaces, while a steward is a persistent identity and operating boundary. Your own docs separate persistent steward identity from the workbench operations the steward uses. The workbench is a composed oRPC service with operations like orient, assess, contribute, govern, and observe, and the principle is that if an agent has to grep, infer, cross-reference, or guess, the workbench failed.


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


So a steward is not primarily “a bag of tools.”
A steward is “an identity plus scope plus memory plus allowed operating surfaces.”


Concretely, I think the right shape is this:


plugins/agents/* stays what you originally meant: cross-runtime skill packs for Claude/Codex/CLI agents.


plugins/channels/* becomes a real thing if you want channel adapters to be first-class in RAWR. That actually fits NanoClaw well, because NanoClaw has WhatsApp built in and expects additional channels like Telegram, Discord, and Slack to be added as code/skills in the fork rather than through a formal plugin API. In other words, NanoClaw does not really give you a plugin system; it gives you a small host that you extend by code, tools, and skills. NanoClaws.io


Then stewards/* becomes the declaration layer.


That gives you three distinct authoring centers:


skill packs: reusable agent capability/instruction/tool overlays

channel plugins: ingress/egress adapters

steward definitions: persistent identities that bind scope, memory, channels, and skill packs together


That is the clean architecture.


So yes, it makes sense for the NanoClaw integration on the RAWR side to mostly consume:


RAWR skill packs

RAWR channel plugins

RAWR steward definitions

RAWR workbench tools


But no, I would not call the steward itself “the NanoClaw plugin.” The steward is higher-level than that.


The simplest concrete mental model is:


RAWR
  defines:
    skill packs
    channel adapters
    steward declarations
    workbench/service tools

agent host
  adapts:
    RAWR declarations -> NanoClaw runtime

NanoClaw
  runs:
    sessions
    channels
    memory
    containers
    long-lived agent execution


That is the right gravity.


What would a steward look like concretely?


Something like this:


export const coordinationSteward = defineSteward({
  id: "coordination-steward",
  displayName: "Coordination Steward",
  domain: {
    scopes: ["@rawr/coordination", "@rawr/coordination-*"],
    agentsMdRoots: ["packages/coordination/AGENTS.md"],
  },
  identity: {
    githubAccount: "rawr-coordination-bot",
    autonomyProfile: "earned",
  },
  memory: {
    kind: "persistent",
    changelog: true,
    decisionHistory: true,
    claudeMdOverlay: "stewards/coordination/CLAUDE.md",
  },
  channels: [
    { ref: "local-dev-console" },
    { ref: "slack-engineering" },
  ],
  skills: [
    { ref: "rawr-core-navigation" },
    { ref: "rawr-contract-authoring" },
    { ref: "coordination-domain-pack" },
  ],
  tools: [
    { ref: "workbench.orient" },
    { ref: "workbench.assess" },
    { ref: "workbench.contribute" },
    { ref: "workbench.govern" },
    { ref: "workbench.observe" },
  ],
  runtime: {
    backend: "nanoclaw",
    containerProfile: "repo-write",
    swarmRole: "lead",
  },
});


That is a steward.


Notice what it is not doing:


it is not implementing Slack

it is not implementing the skill pack

it is not implementing the tool

it is not implementing NanoClaw itself


It is just binding those things into a persistent agent identity.


That is exactly the level you want.


Now the NanoClaw side.


NanoClaw’s actual center of gravity is:


one small process

channels

SQLite-backed persistent state

per-group CLAUDE.md memory

isolated containers

scheduled tasks

tools added directly in code

channels beyond WhatsApp added via code/skills in your fork, not through a real formal plugin API NanoClaws.io


That means the RAWR-to-NanoClaw adapter on the agent host should do translation, not invention.


It should:


register RAWR tools as NanoClaw tools

map RAWR channel plugins into NanoClaw channel handlers

materialize steward-specific memory overlays into NanoClaw’s storage / CLAUDE.md conventions

load steward skill packs into the runtime context

start one or more NanoClaw identities/sessions according to steward declarations


So the authoring center remains RAWR declarations, while NanoClaw is the runtime executor.


That also means your statement “our NanoClaw plugin would basically just be a declarative config” is close, but I would rename it.


It is not really a “NanoClaw plugin.”
It is better thought of as a steward declaration consumed by the agent host’s NanoClaw adapter.


That sounds pedantic, but it matters because NanoClaw itself does not present a serious first-class plugin API. Its own site leans hard on “small enough to understand,” “one process,” “no microservices,” and “no plugin API; just a function in src/tools/.” NanoClaws.io


So the architecture should reflect reality:


NanoClaw is not the plugin host

RAWR is


That is the core correction.


Now, on channels specifically: yes, I think channels should be a separate plugin type if you want this to be portable and pluggable in RAWR. That gives you a stable abstraction independent of NanoClaw, and then the NanoClaw adapter can consume that interface and wire it into NanoClaw’s runtime model. NanoClaw already expects channels to be added in code/skills; you can retrofit that by making your agent host generate or register those bindings from plugins/channels/* instead of hand-coding them every time. NanoClaws.io


So the final shape I would recommend is:


plugins/agents/*      reusable skill packs
plugins/channels/*    ingress/egress adapters
stewards/*            persistent agent declarations
apps/agent-host/*     NanoClaw integration + runtime adapter


And the steward declaration becomes the thing that says:


“I am this persistent agent.
I own this domain.
I can use these skill packs.
I am reachable on these channels.
I keep memory this way.
Run me on NanoClaw.”


That is clean.


The other reason this fits your product docs well is that RAWR is explicitly trying to make autonomous stewardship structural, not vibe-based. The workbench exists so the steward can ask one operation instead of grepping. govern.evaluate exists so autonomy can be earned and mechanically thresholded instead of improvised. observe.changelog exists so the steward can catch up between sessions.


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


That means a steward in RAWR is not just “an agent config.”
It is the system-level identity that ties:


workbench access

trust history

domain scope

channels

memory

runtime


into one persistent boundary.


So yes, the steward should be declarative.
Yes, it should pull in skills, channels, memory bindings, and tool references.
Yes, that is the right place to “declare an agent.”


But do not let that flatten the system into one plugin type.


The clean boundary is:


channels are plugins

skills are plugins/packs

stewards are declarations

NanoClaw is the runtime host backend


That is the model that stays composable instead of collapsing into semantic soup.

source_items.title: Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node
source_items.last_response:
Received app response


No. Keep async host as the primary host semantic.


Do not split it into “async host” and “worker host” as two top-level host concepts unless you already know they are materially different runtime classes.


That split sounds cleaner for five minutes, then it muddies the architecture.


Why it muddies it:


“Async” is the real umbrella runtime role.
It includes:


Inngest durable execution

schedules

event consumers

internal trigger bridges

background jobs

worker-style consumers


“Worker” is only one subtype inside that world.


So if you make worker host peer to async host, you create an overlap problem immediately:


where do Inngest functions live?

where do cron/schedules live?

where do queue consumers that only trigger workflows live?

where do internal async adapters live?


You end up either duplicating semantics or inventing arbitrary rules.


The repo’s current manifest shape already supports the cleaner model: one manifest owns both the workflow trigger surface and one Inngest execution bundle, with the runtime bundle treated as host-owned composition rather than as some separate semantic universe


rawr.hq


. That strongly suggests the right abstraction is a single async runtime host that owns async execution concerns broadly.


So the clean model is:


server host = caller-facing sync boundary host
async host  = non-request execution host
cli host    = command host
web host    = frontend host optional


Inside the async host, you can still have multiple runtime lanes:


async host
  - workflow lane     (Inngest durable execution)
  - scheduler lane    (cron / timed dispatch)
  - consumer lane     (queues / streams / webhooks-to-events)
  - worker lane       (direct background processors when not using Inngest)
  - internal rpc lane (private service routers if needed)


That is the right level of precision.


So “worker” should usually be:


a plugin category, or

a lane inside the async host,
not a separate top-level host semantic.


The leverage/complexity tradeoff is straightforward.


If you create both:


async host

worker host


you now need to explain the difference forever. And the truthful answer will usually be “worker is one kind of async,” which means the split bought you nothing.


The only time I would separate them is if “worker host” becomes a real operational class with distinct requirements such as:


very high-throughput queue consumption

GPU or heavy CPU processing

separate autoscaling policy

different language/runtime

different failure tolerance

no shared relationship to Inngest workflows


At that point, yes, a dedicated worker host can make sense. But then it is not a semantic cleanup move. It is an operational specialization.


So the rule should be:


Keep async host as the canonical host role.
Treat worker as an execution subtype within async.
Promote worker into its own host only when there is an actual operational reason.


That preserves the simple mental model and still leaves room to scale.


The naming I would lock is:


hosts/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/        optional


And then inside async composition:


plugins/workflows/*
plugins/worker/*
plugins/schedules/*   only if you really need it


Or better, even there, keep schedules under workflows unless they are truly non-workflow runtime tasks.


So my blunt answer:


Do not separate “worker host” from “general async host” for semantics alone.
That is taxonomy theater.
Keep one async host.
Let worker be one lane/plugin type inside it.
Split later only for operational reasons.

source_items.title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node
source_items.last_response:
Yes. Then lock that.


If in your architecture services are the real capability center of gravity, then say that plainly and stop softening it.


That means the hierarchy is:


services = capability logic and private callable capability boundaries
plugins = runtime-specific adapters/projections of services
host bundles = composed deployable assemblies of plugins + services
roles = process roles a host bundle can instantiate


So the corrected model is:


services/
  sales/
  todo/
  billing/

plugins/
  server/
  async/
  web/
  agent/
  cli/

apps/roles/
  server/
  async/
  cli/
  web/
  agent/

host-bundles/
  shared/
  sales/
  todo/


And the semantics are:


services/sales/* is where Sales capability actually lives

plugins/server/sales-api/* is just the server-facing adapter for Sales

plugins/async/sales-workflows/* is just the async adapter for Sales

plugins/web/sales-panel/* is just the web adapter for Sales

host-bundles/shared/* composes some or all of those into one deployable bundle


So yes — if services already own the capability directly, then my earlier “packages/ as capability center” framing was too generic for your actual model.


For your model, it should be:


services = capability truth
plugins  = runtime truth
host bundles = deployment/composition truth
roles = process/runtime role truth


That is much sharper.


This also means your host-first plugin split is even more defensible, because plugins are not pretending to be capability roots. They are explicitly runtime adapters over service-owned capability logic.


So your mental model should now be:


A service is not “something that must run remotely.”
It is the canonical capability unit.


A plugin is how that service gets projected into a specific runtime surface.


A host bundle is how a selected set of plugins/services gets assembled into a deployable system.


A role is one process/runtime role inside that bundle.


That also clarifies the transport question:


If services/todo defines the capability, then:


the server plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

the async plugin can call it in-process if mounted locally

either can call it over RPC only if that service boundary is mounted remotely somewhere else


So services being the capability center does not force remote service hosts. It just means the service boundary is the canonical shape.


A good concrete structure would be:


services/
  todo/
    contract.ts
    router.ts
    client.ts
    impl.ts
    schemas.ts

plugins/
  server/
    todo-api/
      manifest.ts
      router.ts

  async/
    todo-workflows/
      manifest.ts
      workflows.ts

    todo-internal/
      manifest.ts
      router.ts

  cli/
    todo-admin/
      manifest.ts
      commands.ts


Here:


services/todo/* owns the Todo capability

plugins/server/todo-api exposes Todo to public server boundaries

plugins/async/todo-workflows exposes Todo execution in async land

plugins/async/todo-internal mounts a private internal router if needed

none of those plugins own Todo’s business truth


That is clean.


So yes, the corrected statement is:


Services is where the capability logic lives directly.


And from that, the final architecture sentence should be:


Services define the capability; plugins adapt it to runtime roles; host bundles compose those adaptations into deployable systems.


That is the version to lock.

source_items.title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node

source_items.title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model
source_items.summary: what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers bun/node
source_items.first_prompt:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node
source_items.last_response:
Very little, by design.


Those folders should be assembly entrypoint folders, not where the real app logic lives.


So apps/<assembly>/<role>/ exists mostly to hold the runtime-specific glue for that assembled host role.


The rule should be:


If something is reusable across assemblies, it does not belong there.
If something is domain/business logic, it does not belong there.
If something is a runtime-entry concern for this exact assembly+role, it probably does.


So in:


apps/hq/server/
apps/hq/async/
apps/sales/server/


the contents should be things like:


main.ts
The actual entrypoint. Boot the role runtime, load the assembly, start the process.


config.ts
Only if this assembly+role has local config shaping that is not generic enough for the host SDK.


env.ts
Only if you want explicit environment parsing/binding local to that role entrypoint.


telemetry.ts
Only if there is role-local telemetry bootstrap that should happen before startup.


routes.ts or mount.ts
Only if you need a tiny local composition wrapper before handing off to the SDK bootstrap.


README.md
Optional, but useful if the role has non-obvious startup/deploy semantics.


That’s basically it.


What should not go there:


service logic

plugin logic

reusable host bootstrap logic

shared schemas

domain workflows

business rules

general utilities


Those belong in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


So the ideal main.ts is almost boring.


Example for server:


import { startServerRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/server";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startServerRole({
  assembly,
});


Example for async:


import { startAsyncRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/async";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startAsyncRole({
  assembly,
});


That is the point. The folder is not there because it is full of code. It is there because it is a stable topology node.


So think of the contents as falling into three buckets.


First, entrypoint glue.
The minimal code needed to start that exact role for that exact assembly.


Second, role-local config/bootstrap.
Env parsing, startup flags, transport bindings, telemetry setup, shutdown hooks.


Third, deployment-adjacent metadata.
Anything specific to how this role is run, built, or deployed.


If a folder has more than that, something is probably leaking into the wrong layer.


A concrete shape could be:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts
      env.ts
      config.ts

    async/
      main.ts
      env.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts


And many of those files may stay tiny for a long time.


Sanity check:


Question: could this file be shared across assemblies?
Yes -> put it in hq-sdk/package/service/plugin layer.

Question: is this file about starting this exact assembly role?
Yes -> it can live in apps/<assembly>/<role>/.


So the blunt answer is:


Almost nothing goes in those folders.
That’s why they’re good.
They are stable runtime entrypoint containers, not logic buckets.

thread_families.summary: Exploratory thread about whether Encore should sit beneath RAWR as an infrastructure substrate, with the conclusion that Railway + Nx is the more pragmatic near-term path while host contracts stay RAWR-owned.

Workflow architecture thread that settles on a reusable workflow shell: public oRPC/OpenAPI control surfaces on one side, Inngest durable execution on the other, with private service clients providing business capability inside workflow steps.

thread_families.canonical_title: Telemetry vs Logging -> NX + RAWR Evolution Sequence
thread_families.summary: Conversation family that starts with observability tooling distinctions, then pivots into a broader architecture and repo-evolution sequence for RAWR, including document creation and service-internal structure.

Deep host/runtime model family covering the distinction between hosts, processes, services, sidecars, runtime roles, service-centric capability ownership, and the eventual minimal assembly-entrypoint shape.

canonicality_matrix.notes: Canonical stance: services own capability truth, plugins project to runtime roles, host bundles compose deployable assemblies, and app role folders stay thin.
Canonical stance: module-local first, service-shared second, package extraction last; shared DB infrastructure is acceptable, shared write authority is not the default.
Canonical stance: workflow plugins expose durable control surfaces rather than mirroring raw function trees.
The family is authoritative within this folder, but no dedicated Markdown doc has yet replaced the conversation as the canonical artifact.
Useful decision record: Encore is deferred in favor of Railway + Nx, but this is an exploratory substrate thread rather than a binding spec.

open_questions.question: Whether a future cross-cloud substrate adapter should exist remains open.
Exact generated router surface details remain an implementation question, not a resolved semantic conflict.
How much of the workflow trigger surface should stay inside workflow plugins versus a generic coordination layer still has some design-space ambiguity.
No standalone canonical observability doc exists yet; observability guidance remains conversation-first.
